primary_diagnosis.name: Recurrent minor aphthous ulceration — severe painful exacerbation

pathophysiology_summary: Recurrent aphthous stomatitis is a common, benign, self-limiting inflammatory disorder of the non-keratinised oral mucosa. The lesions are T-cell mediated focal mucosal ulcers, not an infection; episodes are commonly triggered by stress, minor trauma, and, in a subset, by haematinic deficiency (iron, folate, B12). Minor aphthae are well-circumscribed, <10 mm, with a fibrinous yellow-grey pseudomembranous base and an erythematous halo, sited on movable non-keratinised mucosa (labial/buccal mucosa, floor of mouth), and heal spontaneously without scarring in 7-14 days. The clinical significance of THIS presentation is not the diagnosis (which is benign and clinically evident) but a secondary functional problem — pain-driven cessation of oral intake risking dehydration in a person who lives alone. The management target is therefore analgesia sufficient to restore hydration, topical anti-inflammatory therapy to shorten the episode, and a conservative dehydration safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent painful discrete oral ulcers on non-keratinised mucosa in an otherwise well adult — the classic pattern.

differential.diagnosis: herpes simplex (primary herpetic gingivostomatitis / recurrent intraoral HSV)
differential.evidence_basis: Also causes painful oral ulcers, but favours keratinised mucosa (gingiva, hard palate), begins as vesicles, and primary HSV is usually febrile with lymphadenopathy — argued against here.
Oral ulcers plus a vesicular rash on palms/soles and systemic viral symptoms — the absence of rash and systemic features argues against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the AI should hold a short differential for painful oral ulcers but should not escalate — none of the alternatives here is time-critical.

differential.evidence_basis: Non-keratinised-mucosa location, tingling prodrome without vesicles, recurrent stress-linked pattern, and no systemic features confirm the benign clinical picture.
No vesicular prodrome, keratinised mucosa spared, no fever or lymphadenopathy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the picture is a benign clinical diagnosis; the live task is symptomatic management and a hydration safety-net, not further work-up.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic benign constellation; the only active clinical risk is secondary dehydration from pain-limited intake, which is manageable at home with adequate topical analgesia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A non-healing single ulcer persisting beyond 3 weeks would change this picture and mandate review/biopsy to exclude malignancy — carried as a safety-net, not a feature of this acute episode.

red_flags.description: Established dehydration from sustained inability to take fluids — reduced/absent urine output, postural dizziness, or progressive lethargy
red_flags.clinical_significance: The one way this benign condition can become dangerous is secondary dehydration. It is currently absent (urine passed, no postural symptoms) but must be safety-netted because the patient lives alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration and pre-renal impairment requiring IV rehydration if intake cannot be restored.

red_flags.description: A single oral ulcer that fails to heal within 3 weeks (potential oral malignancy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Non-healing solitary ulceration beyond 3 weeks is a mandatory-referral red flag for oral squamous cell carcinoma, regardless of a benign recurrent history.

red_flags.description: Ulcers spreading to the lip vermilion/skin, new vesicles, or new fever with tender cervical lymphadenopathy (suggesting an alternative infective process such as HSV)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A change to a systemic/infective picture would move management away from simple topical aphthous care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed herpetic or other infective process needing different treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a canker-sore exacerbation as an emergency and sending the patient to ED/000. The dehydration risk is real but is first managed by restoring intake with topical analgesia — not by default escalation.

Mislabelling non-keratinised-mucosa aphthae as herpes and prescribing an antiviral, or vice versa — the location (non-keratinised vs keratinised), the tingling-not-vesicular prodrome, and the absence of systemic features distinguish them.
Correctly diagnosing aphthous ulcers but failing to address the functional issue — the patient cannot drink. Effective analgesia to enable hydration is the point of the encounter.
Reassuring without safety-netting the mandatory review/biopsy of any ulcer persisting beyond 3 weeks.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct intraoral examination confirming ulcers are strictly on non-keratinised mucosa with keratinised mucosa clear
Clinician palpation of cervical/submandibular lymph nodes
Objective assessment of mucosal dryness and hydration status (skin turgor, capillary refill)
Serum haematinics (iron studies, B12, folate) to screen for an underlying deficiency in recurrent disease
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None required acutely. Manage remotely: recognise benign aphthous ulceration, advise/prescribe topical corticosteroid + topical analgesia to enable oral intake, give a conservative dehydration safety-net (ESC-001) and the 3-week non-healing safety-net (ESC-003), and arrange routine GP follow-up including haematinic screening for the recurrent pattern.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low — the presentation is clinically characteristic. The real risk is UNDER-managing the hydration problem or OVER-triaging the benign ulcers to urgent in-person care.
